preamble.summary: Psalm 115 is a liturgical psalm that moves from a plea for God's glory to a devastating satire on idols, then to a threefold call for Israel to trust the LORD, and finally to a blessing and a declaration of praise. The psalm contrasts the living God who acts from heaven with the dead idols who have mouths but cannot speak, eyes but cannot see, ears but cannot hear. The worshipers of those idols, the psalmist warns, become like them.
preamble.remarkable: The idol satire in verses 4-8 is among the most cutting passages in the Hebrew Bible. The psalmist catalogs the sensory organs of the idols — mouths, eyes, ears, noses, hands, feet, throats — and declares each one non-functional. The idols are a complete inversion of life: they have the form of a living being but none of the substance. The devastating conclusion in verse 8 is that the makers and worshipers of these idols 'become like them' — they too lose the capacity to speak, see, hear, and act. Idolatry does not merely offend God; it dehumanizes the worshiper. You become what you worship.
preamble.friction: The psalm's sharp division between Israel (who trusts the LORD) and the nations (who make idols) can feel exclusivist to modern readers. However, the psalm's critique is directed at the objects of worship, not at the worshipers as persons — the concern is that idol worship degrades human beings by orienting them toward dead things. The statement that 'the dead do not praise the LORD' (v. 17) reflects the Hebrew Bible's general uncertainty about the state of the dead, where Sheol is often described as a place of silence rather than of worship.
preamble.connections: Psalm 115 is part of the Egyptian Hallel (113-118), sung after the Passover meal. The idol satire parallels Isaiah 44:9-20 and Jeremiah 10:1-16. The threefold call to trust (Israel, house of Aaron, those who fear the LORD) appears also in Psalm 118:2-4 and Psalm 135:19-20. The 'you become like what you worship' principle is echoed in Romans 1:21-23 where Paul describes humanity exchanging the glory of God for images.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֤א לָ֥נוּ יְהוָ֗ה לֹ֫א לָ֥נוּ כִּֽי־לְ֭שִׁמְךָ תֵּ֣ן כָּב֑וֹד עַל־חַ֝סְדְּךָ֗ עַל־אֲמִתֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Not unto us, O LORD, not unto us, but unto thy name give glory, for thy mercy, and for thy truth's sake.
verses.rendering:
Not to us, LORD, not to us,
but to Your name give glory,
for the sake of Your faithful love,
for the sake of Your faithfulness.
verses.expanded_rendering: Lo lanu YHWH lo lanu ('not to us, LORD, not to us') — the double negation is emphatic. The psalmist refuses to claim glory for Israel and redirects it entirely to God's shem ('name'). The reasons are ki le-shimkha ten kavod ('for to Your name give glory') — the glory belongs to God's revealed character. The basis is dual: al chasdekha ('because of Your faithful love') and al amittekha ('because of Your faithfulness'). The psalm begins not with human need but with divine reputation — the concern is that God be honored for who He is.
translator_notes: The word chesed is rendered 'faithful love' — it carries the sense of covenant loyalty, committed kindness, and steadfast devotion. The word emet here functions as 'faithfulness' or 'reliability' rather than abstract 'truth.' Together, chesed and emet form one of the most important word pairs in the Hebrew Bible, describing the twin pillars of God's covenant character.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, covenant loyalty, steadfast kindness, lovingkindness, devotion
key_terms.note: chesed is the Hebrew Bible's most important relational word. It describes love that is bound by commitment — not merely an emotion but an obligation freely assumed and faithfully maintained. When God acts in chesed, He acts from covenant loyalty.
When paired with chesed, emet functions as 'faithfulness' — the reliability dimension of God's covenant character. God's chesed is not fickle; it is backed by emet, by a firmness that does not waver.

verses.text_kjv: Wherefore should the heathen say, Where is now their God?
verses.rendering:
Why should the nations say,
"Where is their God?"
translator_notes: The taunt ayyeh na Eloheihem ('where, then, is their God?') is a challenge to God's existence or power. The nations look at Israel's circumstances and conclude that Israel's God is absent or impotent. The psalmist raises this taunt not to validate it but to set up the answer that follows.

verses.text_kjv: But our God is in the heavens: he hath done whatsoever he hath pleased.
verses.rendering:
But our God is in the heavens;
He does whatever He pleases.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ve-Eloheinu va-shamayim ('but our God is in the heavens') — the answer to the nations' taunt is not defensive but declarative. God is not absent; He is in the heavens, the place of supreme authority. And kol asher chafetz asah ('everything He desires, He does') — His location in the heavens is not distance but sovereignty. He is not far away; He is above, acting freely according to His own will. The contrast with the idols about to be described is sharp: God does what He pleases; idols can do nothing at all.
translator_notes: The verb chafetz ('He desires, He pleases') is the same root used in Psalm 112:1 for the person who 'delights' in God's commandments. The God of Israel is characterized by effective will — He desires and He does. This is the fundamental contrast with idols, which have no will and no capacity to act.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲ֭צַבֵּיהֶם כֶּ֣סֶף וְזָהָ֑ב מַ֝עֲשֵׂ֗ה יְדֵ֣י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Their idols are silver and gold, the work of men's hands.
verses.rendering:
Their idols are silver and gold,
crafted by human hands.
translator_notes: The word atzabbeihem ('their idols') comes from the root atzav, which can mean 'to shape, to form' but also 'to grieve, to cause pain.' The idols are shaped objects that cause grief. They are kesef ve-zahav ('silver and gold') — valuable materials, but still materials. And they are ma'aseh yedei adam ('the work of human hands') — made by the very creatures they are supposed to serve. The maker is greater than the made; the worshiper has degraded himself by bowing to his own handiwork.

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּֽה־לָ֭הֶם וְלֹ֣א יְדַבֵּ֑רוּ עֵינַ֥יִם לָ֝הֶ֗ם וְלֹ֣א יִרְאֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They have mouths, but they speak not: eyes have they, but they see not:
verses.rendering:
They have mouths but cannot speak;
they have eyes but cannot see.
translator_notes: The satire begins its catalog. The peh ('mouth') is the organ of speech, of command, of blessing — the idols have the form but not the function. The einayim ('eyes') are the organs of perception, of attention, of care — the idols have the shape but not the sight. Each line follows the same devastating pattern: they have X but cannot do what X does.

verses.text_hebrew: אָזְנַ֣יִם לָ֭הֶם וְלֹ֣א יִשְׁמָ֑עוּ אַ֥ף לָ֝הֶ֗ם וְלֹ֣א יְרִיחֽוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: They have ears, but they hear not: noses have they, but they smell not:
verses.rendering:
They have ears but cannot hear;
they have noses but cannot smell.
translator_notes: The catalog continues. The oznayim ('ears') cannot shama ('hear') — the God of Israel hears prayer, but these objects cannot receive any communication. The af ('nose') cannot reach ('smell') — the nose was particularly significant in ancient Near Eastern worship because incense offerings were understood as a pleasing aroma to the deity. An idol with a nose that cannot smell cannot receive the very offerings placed before it.

verses.text_hebrew: יְדֵיהֶ֤ם ׀ וְלֹ֬א יְמִישׁ֗וּן רַ֭גְלֵיהֶם וְלֹ֣א יְהַלֵּ֑כוּ לֹֽא־יֶ֝הְגּ֗וּ בִּגְרוֹנָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: They have hands, but they handle not: feet have they, but they walk not: neither speak they through their throat.
translator_notes: The catalog reaches its climax. The yadayim ('hands') cannot yamish ('feel, touch, handle') — they cannot act, cannot intervene, cannot rescue. The raglayim ('feet') cannot halakh ('walk') — they cannot come to anyone's aid, cannot move toward the one who prays. The final line is the most damning: lo yehgu bigronam ('they do not murmur in their throat') — they cannot even produce the most basic sound. The garon ('throat') that should produce voice produces nothing. Total silence. Total impotence.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּ֭מוֹהֶם יִהְי֣וּ עֹשֵׂיהֶ֑ם כֹּ֖ל אֲשֶׁר־בֹּטֵ֣חַ בָּהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: They that make them are like unto them; so is every one that trusteth in them.
verses.rendering:
Those who make them become like them —
so does everyone who trusts in them.
verses.expanded_rendering: Kemochem yihyu oseihem ('like them will be those who make them') — this is the psalm's theological thesis stated with devastating brevity. The idol-maker becomes like the idol: unable to speak, see, hear, smell, feel, walk, or produce sound. Worship shapes the worshiper into the image of what is worshiped. If you worship something dead, you become progressively less alive. If you worship something that cannot see or hear, you lose your own capacity for perception. The principle works in reverse too: those who worship the living God become more alive, more perceptive, more capable of action.
translator_notes: The verb yihyu ('they will become') is future — the transformation is progressive and ongoing. The principle stated here is foundational: you become what you worship. This is not a curse imposed from outside but an organic consequence of misdirected devotion.

verses.text_hebrew: יִ֭שְׂרָאֵל בְּטַ֣ח בַּיהוָ֑ה עֶזְרָ֖ם וּמָגִנָּ֣ם הֽוּא׃
verses.text_kjv: O Israel, trust thou in the LORD: he is their help and their shield.
verses.rendering:
Israel, trust in the LORD!
He is their help and their shield.
translator_notes: The threefold call to trust begins. Israel as a whole is addressed first. The verb betach ('trust') is an imperative — this is a command, not a suggestion. The LORD is described as ezram u-maginnam ('their help and their shield') — active assistance and protective covering. The shift from second person ('trust') to third person ('their help') suggests a liturgical antiphon: one voice calls, another responds.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ית אַ֭הֲרֹן בִּטְח֣וּ בַיהוָ֑ה עֶזְרָ֖ם וּמָגִנָּ֣ם הֽוּא׃
verses.text_kjv: O house of Aaron, trust in the LORD: he is their help and their shield.
verses.rendering:
House of Aaron, put your trust in the LORD!
He is their help and their shield.
translator_notes: The second call addresses beit Aharon ('house of Aaron') — the priestly families who serve in the Temple. Even those closest to God's presence need the command to trust. The refrain is identical: ezram u-maginnam hu ('He is their help and their shield').

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאֵ֣י יְ֭הוָה בִּטְח֣וּ בַיהוָ֑ה עֶזְרָ֖ם וּמָגִנָּ֣ם הֽוּא׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye that fear the LORD, trust in the LORD: he is their help and their shield.
verses.rendering:
You who fear the LORD, put your trust in the LORD!
He is their help and their shield.
translator_notes: The third call addresses yir'ei YHWH ('those who fear the LORD') — a category broader than ethnic Israel, potentially including God-fearing non-Israelites. This three-part structure (Israel, house of Aaron, God-fearers) creates concentric circles of covenant community. The refrain remains unchanged: the help and shield are the same for all who trust.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֥ה זְכָרָ֗נוּ יְבָ֫רֵ֥ךְ יְ֭בָרֵךְ אֶת־בֵּ֣ית יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל יְ֝בָרֵ֗ךְ אֶת־בֵּ֥ית אַהֲרֹֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD hath been mindful of us: he will bless us; he will bless the house of Israel; he will bless the house of Aaron.
translator_notes: The verb zekharanu ('He has remembered us') shifts from command to confidence — after calling for trust, the psalm now affirms that God is already acting. The threefold yevarekh ('He will bless') mirrors the threefold call to trust. God's blessing follows the same three-part structure: Israel, house of Aaron, and (in the next verse) those who fear the LORD.

verses.text_kjv: He will bless them that fear the LORD, both small and great.
verses.rendering:
He will bless those who fear the LORD,
the small and the great alike.
translator_notes: The blessing extends to yir'ei YHWH ('those who fear the LORD') — completing the threefold pattern. The phrase ha-qetannim im ha-gedolim ('the small with the great') emphasizes that God's blessing is not proportional to human status. The servant and the king, the child and the elder, the insignificant and the powerful all receive the same divine attention.

verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall increase you more and more, you and your children.
verses.rendering:
May the LORD give you increase,
you and your children.
translator_notes: The verb yosef ('may He add, may He increase') is a jussive expressing a blessing-wish. The increase is aleikhem ('upon you') and al beneikhem ('and upon your children') — the blessing is generational, extending to descendants. This echoes the Abrahamic promise of multiplication.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרוּכִ֣ים אַ֭תֶּם לַיהוָ֑ה עֹ֝שֵׂ֗ה שָׁמַ֥יִם וָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye are blessed of the LORD which made heaven and earth.
verses.rendering:
You are blessed by the LORD,
the maker of heaven and earth.
translator_notes: Berukhim attem la-YHWH ('blessed are you by the LORD') — the passive participle declares the blessing as already in effect. The LORD is identified as oseh shamayim va-aretz ('maker of heaven and earth') — a creation title that contrasts sharply with the idols who are ma'aseh yedei adam ('the work of human hands'). The creator God blesses; the created idols cannot.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשָּׁמַ֣יִם שָׁ֭מַיִם לַיהוָ֑ה וְ֝הָאָ֗רֶץ נָתַ֥ן לִבְנֵי־אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The heaven, even the heavens, are the LORD's: but the earth hath he given to the children of men.
verses.rendering:
The heavens belong to the LORD,
but the earth He has given to the children of humanity.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ha-shamayim shamayim la-YHWH ('the heavens — heavens belong to the LORD') — the doubled shamayim is emphatic, claiming the entire celestial realm as God's domain. Ve-ha-aretz natan livnei adam ('but the earth He has given to the children of Adam') — the earth is a gift, entrusted to humanity. The verse establishes a distinction between God's realm (heavens) and humanity's realm (earth) without suggesting that God is absent from the earth — rather, the earth is the sphere where human beings exercise the stewardship God has granted.
translator_notes: This verse is sometimes read as limiting God's involvement with earth, but in the context of a psalm that has just affirmed God's active blessing, provision, and increase on earth, the point is not divine absence but human responsibility. The earth is given — it is a trust, not an abandonment.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֣א הַ֭מֵּתִים יְהַלְלוּ־יָ֑הּ וְ֝לֹ֗א כָּל־יֹרְדֵ֥י דוּמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The dead praise not the LORD, neither any that go down into silence.
verses.rendering:
The dead do not praise the LORD,
nor do any who go down into silence.
translator_notes: Lo ha-metim yehalelu Yah ('the dead do not praise the LORD') — in the Hebrew understanding of death, Sheol is a place of silence, inactivity, and cessation of worship. The phrase yordei dumah ('those who go down into silence') is a poetic name for the dead and for the realm of death. Dumah means 'silence' — the opposite of praise. This verse functions as motivation: praise God now, because the dead cannot.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנַ֤חְנוּ ׀ נְבָ֘רֵ֤ךְ יָ֗הּ מֵעַתָּ֥ה וְעַד־עוֹלָ֗ם הַֽלְלוּ־יָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: But we will bless the LORD from this time forth and for evermore. Praise the LORD.
verses.rendering:
But we will bless the LORD
from now until forever.
Praise the LORD!
translator_notes: Va-anachnu nevarekh Yah ('but we — we will bless the LORD') — the emphatic pronoun anachnu sets up a contrast with the dead of the previous verse. The dead are silent; we will speak. The phrase me'attah ve-ad olam ('from now until forever') matches Psalm 113:2, creating a frame around the Hallel collection. The final Hallelujah closes the psalm with the word that opened it.
